text.title: Terraform Remote Backends
text.summary: Backend options comparison, S3/GCS/Azure/Terraform Cloud configuration rules, and state security requirements.

section.title: Backend Selection

rule.statement: Always use a remote backend for any team or CI environment; never use local state in production; choose S3+DynamoDB for AWS-centric teams, GCS for GCP-centric teams.
rule.rationale: Local state blocks collaboration — two engineers running apply concurrently corrupt state. Remote backends with locking serialize operations safely.

section.note: Backend comparison: S3+DynamoDB (AWS, native locking, SSE-KMS), GCS (GCP, native locking, default encryption), Azure Blob (Azure, AAD auth), Terraform Cloud (multi-cloud, policy enforcement, free tier ended March 2026).

rule.statement: Configure S3 backend with encrypt = true, KMS key for compliance workloads, and DynamoDB table for locking; use key path pattern {env}/{component}/terraform.tfstate.
rule.rationale: KMS encryption satisfies SOC2/PCI compliance requirements. The env/component key path enables state isolation per environment and blast-radius separation per component.

rule.statement: Provision state infrastructure (S3 bucket, DynamoDB table, KMS key, IAM policy) as a separate one-time bootstrapped Terraform configuration, not in the main project.
rule.rationale: State infrastructure must exist before any other Terraform configuration can initialize. Bootstrapping it separately avoids a chicken-and-egg dependency.

rule.statement: Enable versioning on the S3 state bucket; set a lifecycle rule to expire non-current versions after 90 days; set prevent_destroy on the bucket and DynamoDB table.
rule.rationale: Versioning enables rollback after state corruption. 90-day retention balances recovery capability with storage cost. prevent_destroy prevents accidental bootstrap teardown.

rule.statement: Restrict state bucket access to Terraform CI/CD roles only via IAM; enable access logging on the state bucket; use OIDC authentication in CI rather than static access keys.
rule.rationale: State files contain passwords, connection strings, and private keys. Broad read access to state is equivalent to broad access to all managed infrastructure.

rule.statement: Mark sensitive outputs with sensitive = true; use external secret managers (Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) rather than storing secrets in Terraform variables that land in state.
rule.rationale: Even with KMS encryption, minimizing secrets in state reduces blast radius if the state bucket is compromised.

antipattern.description: Local tfstate files in git repositories.
antipattern.reason: State contains plaintext credentials and resource IDs. Once committed, secrets cannot be fully purged from git history.
